agent1_snippets.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: Patients with latent TB are asymptomatic. Patients with early active TB are also often asymptomatic, with no specific physical findings. As early active TB progresses, patients may experience the insidious onset of cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss, and hemoptysis before seeking medical evaluation. Depending on the extent of the disease, the physical findings on lung examination may be normal or demonstrate areas of consolidation, airway inflammation, or cavities. Patients with chronic, extensive, destructive cavities with surrounding fibrosis may develop chest wall deformities due to loss of underlying lung volume. [31] Extrapulmonary TB can involve any organ, resulting in myriad physical findings beyond the scope of this document (see Image. Clinical Features of Extrapulmonary Tuberculosis).

agent1_snippets.title: Pneumoconiosis -- Clinical Pathology
agent1_snippets.content: The symptoms of pneumoconiosis tend to be nonspecific and may overlap with other pulmonary comorbidities such as chronic bronchitis, COPD, and emphysema. A thorough occupational history should be taken, exploring both the exposure and duration to harmful inhalants. Patients may complain of shortness of breath, decreased exercise tolerance, gradual onset of a nonproductive cough, or might be asymptomatic with only an abnormal chest X-ray. In the case of coal workers’ pneumoconiosis, black-pigmented sputum may be produced.  On physical exam, tachypnea and end-inspiratory crackles may be noted. Friction rubbing or wheezing may be heard when auscultating lungs. [11] Cardiac auscultation may reveal accentuation of P2 at the left upper sternal border.

agent1_snippets.content: No uniform guidelines exist for diagnosing miliary TB, but the following criteria have been suggested: Clinical presentation consistent with a diagnosis of TB, such as pyrexia with evening temperature rise, weight loss, anorexia, tachycardia, and night sweats of greater than 6 weeks duration responding to antitubercular treatment Classical miliary pattern on chest radiograph Bilateral diffuse reticulonodular lung lesions on a background of miliary shadows demonstrable either on plain chest radiograph or high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) Microbiological, cytopathological, histopathological, or molecular evidence of TB

agent1_snippets.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- History and Physical -- Pleural disease
agent1_snippets.content: Occasionally, pleural disease is seen in pediatric TB and may happen without segmental or miliary lesions in the lung parenchyma. This occurs when bacilli form a subpleural focus and erode into the pleural space. A hypersensitivity response ensues, stimulating the formation of a pleural effusion. Due to the brisk immune response, these collections may become large enough to be associated with abrupt onset of fever, chest pain, and shortness of breath with rapid respiratory compromise depending on the child's size and relative pleural effusion size. The physical examination will reveal dullness to percussion and diminished breath sounds on the affected side. Despite appropriate treatment, fevers are often very high and may persist for several weeks. [23] [24] Microbiologic confirmation is often difficult due to low numbers of bacteria. [25] Pleural biopsy may demonstrate granuloma formation but requires surgical intervention or a fine needle biopsy. Newer techniques, such as polymerase chain reaction, may prove useful in such situations, but TB detection rates still fall below 50%. [26] Because true empyema rarely forms, it must be considered in the differential diagnosis of any culture-negative pleural specimen, especially in high-burden countries. [26]

agent1_snippets.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Screening should be done with the intent to treat if the result is positive. [13] Chest radiography should be obtained. [13] If there are findings that could be consistent with active pulmonary tuberculosis, such as cavitation, airspace opacities, pleural effusions, or fibrotic changes, active pulmonary tuberculosis should be ruled out with 3 serial acid-fast bacilli (AFB) sputum cultures collected 8 hours apart with one being an early morning sample. [13] If all 3 AFB sputum smears are negative, active tuberculosis has been ruled out, and testing and offering tuberculosis-preventative therapy for LTBI can be considered. [13] Ziehl-Neelsen stain is often utilized for this smear, which employs carbolfuchsin and methylene blue dyes. [24]

agent1_snippets.title: Miliary Tuberculosis -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: The clinical features of miliary TB vary widely, depending on the sites mainly involved, and may remain obscure until late in the disease course. Initial symptoms develop gradually and are usually nonspecific, which include constitutional manifestations such as fever, generalized weakness, anorexia, weight loss, and lassitude. Many patients may initially present with productive coughing, difficulty breathing, chest pain, and hemoptysis. Abdominal symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain are also common in the early stages.

agent1_snippets.title: Emphysema -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: In the early stages, percussion findings may be normal. As the disease progresses and airway obstruction increases, physical examination findings may range from prolonged expiration or wheezes during forced exhalation to increased resonance, indicating hyperinflation. On auscultation, distant breath sounds, wheezes, crackles at the lung bases, or distant heart sounds may be heard.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
agent2_snippets.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
agent2_snippets.content: Abces van long en mediastinum, a form of خراج (abscess formation) that belongs to the category of other lower respiratory disease, is linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder) and frequently manifests as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass) within the mediastinum. The mediastinum, situated in the Subdivision of thorax, serves as a common anatomical site for direct procedural findings on thoracic CT imaging, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas that may be unilateral (right or left), bilateral, or localized. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary diagnostic modality for detecting a spectrum of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities such as mass of the mid mediastinum, mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, mediastinal effusion, and miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Mediastinal effusion is classified as a type of اضطراب منصفي. Various CT techniques are employed, ranging from CT angiography of thorax with contrast (a specific ct scan chest isa) to diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, highlighting the breadth of imaging options. Findings are reported through computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and related ct scan chest subtypes. Therapeutically, closed drainage of chest is performed within the thoracic anatomical framework to manage these pathologies. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how lower respiratory diseases, particularly those involving abscesses, can involve the mediastinum; how mediastinal pathology—including masses, effusions, calcifications, diffuse infiltrates, and mediastinal‑pleural communications—is visualized and classified through diverse CT modalities; and how procedural interventions such as closed chest drainage are integrated into patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
agent2_snippets.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) belongs to the broader category “إجراءات تصوير متعلقة بمنطقة الصدر والسبيل التنفسي” (imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract) and includes several procedural variants: “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material,” its extended protocol with subsequent contrast, and “Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast.” These CT modalities are the primary imaging procedures used to evaluate mediastinal pathology. agent2_snippets.content: The mediastinum is a central thoracic subdivision that serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory conditions classified under “other lower respiratory disease” (اضطراب منصفي), including mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), lung‑mediastinal abscesses, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Chest CT—performed as a diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that may begin without contrast and continue with contrast, or as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast—provides the “has_finding_method” link to a variety of mediastinal findings: mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Chest CT also identifies mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, recorded with the “has_direct_procedure_site” relationship to the mediastinum, and specifies the laterality of the fistula (bilateral, right‑sided, left‑sided). Therapeutically, closed drainage of the chest is associated with the thoracic subdivision and may be employed to manage complications such as mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. Together, the anatomical context of the mediastinum, its disease categorization, the detailed CT imaging techniques, the specific radiologic findings, and the related interventional procedures constitute an integrated framework for diagnosing and treating mediastinal and thoracic disorders.
